figures.caption: TABLE III: Search results: top configurations by accuracy and Pareto optimal solutions. Rows marked • are Pareto optimal over (F1, F2, F3); unmarked rows are top accuracy configurations. Angle-y with CNOT mode “none-odd” dominates both categories.

Fig. 1: Comparison of QAS without F3 versus with F3 on MNIST: (a) Bar chart showing average number of cuts (for Qtarget=4) across 5% accuracy bins (solutions with accuracy ≥ 65%). (b) Cumulative distribution function (CDF) of circuit cost (F2) showing percentage of solutions with F2 ≤ x. The “With F3” approach reduces both cutting overhead and circuit cost while maintaining similar accuracy.
Fig. 15: Circuit cutting for the best 8q-2d angle-y architecture (F3=5): 5 subcircuits for a 4 qubit device. SC1-2: embedding and Layer 1; SC3-4: Layer 2; SC5: cross group CNOTs and measurements.
TABLE IX: Iris search results: top configurations by accuracy and selected Pareto optimal solutions. Amplitude embedding dominates; best accuracy is 4q-2d with none-odd.
Fig. 4: NAS pipeline. Data and objectives feed a NAS engine that evolves DNN architectures via a supernet and accuracy predictor.
Fig. 5: QNAS pipeline with NSGA-II. See Sec. III for details.
Fig. 3: Hybrid quantum classical neural network used in QNAS.

Fig. 9: NSGA-II selection mechanism. Left: Parent population Pt. Middle: Combined population Pt ∪ Qt sorted into non dominated fronts (darker = better front). Right: Selection of Pt+1 from top fronts; the dashed line indicates where crowding distance breaks ties within a partially selected front.
